dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to 3-11 for respiratory distress at 7400 [CITY].
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Uh, shortness of breath. It's been getting worse for two days.
Are you experiencing any chest pain or dizziness?
Nope. Just feel like I'm not getting air. I've been using my inhaler, but it's not helping.
Can you tell me your name?
Yeah, it's Mike.
What county are you in?
[STATE]. Wait, I mean, [CITY] County.
What year is it?
2019, I think.
Good. What happened today?
I had to call 911 because I could not get air. I also had some nausea and chills.
He is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Eyes are open and focused.
Do you mind if we take a look at your airway, Mr. Mike?
No, go ahead.
Your airway is open and self-maintained. That's good.
Do you mind if we put on the pulse ox and listen to your lungs?
Respirations are 33 a minute, non-labored. SpO2 is 97% at room air after walking to the stretcher.
Taking a quick listen to his lungs. We've got diminished sounds at the bases with some wheezing in all lobes.
Let me check his pulse and take his blood pressure.
Pulse is strong and regular. Let me check peripheral pulses.
Pulse is 96 and strong.
Blood pressure is 151 over 86.
Can you describe the shortness of breath in more detail?
It's been for two days now. Started subtly, but now it's worse. I hadn't been able to sleep well. I also had nausea and chills.
You mentioned using your inhaler. Has it helped at all?
A little relief, but not as much as before. Normally takes longer but I haven't had much effect. I'm on 3 LPM of O2 today, but my normal is 2 LPM.
What's your medical history?
I have COPD and a history of kidney stones.
What medications do you take regularly?
Albuterol, and furosemide. I've also been on oxygen at home.
Any allergies?
I am allergic to iodine and IV contrast dye.
Was there anything unusual you ate or drank the day before?
No, not really. It's my breathing that's the issue.
Taking a closer look at the chest area and re-listening to lungs.
Still hearing wheezing throughout. No trauma or deformity seen. Grunting noted during expiration to assist breathing.
Respiratory rate has slowed a bit to 25 a minute now.
We're going to place you on the cardiac monitor and do a four-lead EKG.
The four-lead EKG shows a normal sinus rhythm now.
You're already on your nasal cannula set to 3 LPM. Would you mind if we administered a nebulizer with albuterol and atrovent?
Please do. It's the only thing that helps me now.
SpO2 is up to 98% now thanks to the oxygen support.
Blood pressure is now 124 over 64. Pulse is down to 88.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you describe your shortness of breath?
It went from a 9 to about a 2 after the treatment. No pain anywhere else. I still grunt, but it helps me.
Mike is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. GCS is 15 - E4V5M6.
Your pulse is still 88. That's a bit fast, but we'll keep an eye on it.
We're going to start an IV to administer the dexamethasone.
Given the signs and EKG, we're treating this under protocol 3-11 for COPD exacerbation and bronchospasm acute onset.
EKG remains in normal sinus rhythm with no sign of STEMI.
Blood glucose is up to 127 mg/dL now.
Let's confirm a 12-lead EKG to track for any changes.
Same reading. No change - still normal for a 60-year-old male.
Let's make sure the dexamethasone hasn't caused any issues or increased your shortness of breath.
Blood pressure is back up to 147 over 69 and his pulse back to 96.
How do you feel after the dexamethasone?
I feel more comfortable now. I wasn't dizzy for long. Grunting still helps me out.
We need to move you onto the stretcher now. Do you think you can stand on your own?
I can make it, as long as I get my breath.
He is transportable. Let's help you over to the stretcher.
You mentioned a tachycardia prior with a pulse of 96. Let's double-check the rhythm now.
What hospital would you like to go to?
[COMPANY_NAME].
Secured on the stretcher with five-point restraints, a blanket, and a sheet.
His SpO2 remained at 98%. He's stable breathing with the nebulizer off.
Fowler's position applied to help his breathing. End-tidal CO2 started and he's on the four-lead monitor.
He is alert and oriented, with no changes in mental status. GCS is 15.
He also has an allergy to iodine and IV contrast dye. Please note that at the hospital.
He denied chest pain and remains with no pain on scale. We'll track for any new symptoms.
SpO2 increased to 99% enroute after administering the nebulizer and dexamethasone.
He has a prior history of CPAP use during a panic attack, but says this does not feel like one.
All treatments and medications administered as per protocol. Patient is stable with regard to mental status and no new complaints.
His SpO2 has maintained above 97%. Dexamethasone and nebulizer improved his breathing significantly.
Patient earlier mentioned chills. Stable and comfortable now.
No distress noted. He spoke clearly and moved to the stretcher on his own.
IV site looks stable. Maintaining oxygen at 3 LPM during transport.
All clear. Voyager 327 en route to [COMPANY_NAME] with a COPD exacerbation.
His shortness of breath is improving, and he can breathe and talk better now. Let me adjust the stretcher.
Radioing ahead to the ER nurse that we will drop him in ED25 on the RAA.
